raw.parent_tribe: Quraysh - Hāshim (Hz. Peygamber'in amcası al-'Abbās'ın soyundan)
raw.regions_all: Irak; İran; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir (başlangıçta); Kahire (sonraki dönem)
raw.narrative_en: The Abbasids built the golden age of Islamic civilization and remained the symbolic center of the Sunni world for five centuries. They founded Baghdad, transforming it into the era's greatest intellectual and commercial hub where the Bayt al-Hikma translated Greek, Indian, and Persian knowledge into Arabic. The reigns of Harun al-Rashid and al-Ma'mun represented the zenith, while from the 10th century real power passed to military dynasties like the Buyids and Seljuks. The shadow caliphate in Cairo served a symbolic function, legitimizing Mamluk sultans.

raw.regions_all: İfrîkıye; Mısır; Güney Suriye; Filistin; Sicilya; Hicaz; Yemen (davet)
raw.narrative_en: The Fatimids were a dynasty founded on the Ismaili Shia caliphal claim that created Cairo as its capital, building an alternative Islamic center to the Abbasids. They founded al-Azhar, leaving one of the Islamic world's most enduring educational institutions; Druzism took shape under al-Hakim, while the Nizari-Musta'li split determined the future of the Ismaili movement.

raw.capital: Kahire (Mısır); Şam (Suriye); Halep; Hama; Hıms; Mayyāfāriqīn; Hısn Keyfā
raw.region_primary: Mısır, Suriye, Diyâr-ı Bekr, Cezire, Yemen
raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Diyâr-ı Bekr; Cezire; Yemen; Hicaz
raw.narrative_en: The Ayyubids were the dynasty founded by the Kurdish-origin Saladin after abolishing the Fatimid caliphate, recapturing Jerusalem from the Crusaders at Hattin. Their confederative structure uniting Egypt and Syria symbolized the resurgence of Sunni Islam, though this decentralization eventually led to their fragmentation.

names.ja: アール・アッ＝シャイフ

raw.notes: Descendants of Husayn ibn Ali
Banu Hashim cadet branches ruled Mecca for ~1000 years
Sayyid lineage ruled Medina
Already listed - religious counterpart to House of Saud
Sharifs of Asir; living Sayyid lineage

raw.regions_all: Hicaz; Suriye; Filistin; Mısır; Irak; İran; Bahreyn; Umman; Yemen
raw.narrative_en: The Rashidun Caliphate represents the founding political order of the Islamic community after Prophet Muhammad's death, dismantling the Sasanian Empire entirely and expelling Byzantium from Syria and Egypt within three decades. Abu Bakr secured Arabian unity through the Ridda Wars, while Umar transformed the conquests into a systematic state apparatus. Uthman standardized the Quranic text, but the civil strife (fitna) under Ali created the defining political fracture in Islamic history.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Carmathian or Qarmati Rulers of the line of Abū Sa'īd al-Jannābī
raw.regions_all: al-Ahsā; Bahreyn; Güney Irak (geçici); Suriye sınırı (geçici); Umân (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Qarmatians represented the most radical branch of Ismaili Shiism, a militant movement that shook the Islamic world by stealing the Black Stone from the Kaaba. Their egalitarian community model established in eastern Arabia, while controversial, constituted one of the most provocative experiments in Islamic political thought.

raw.regions_all: Umân; Maskat; Zengibar; Doğu Afrika kıyıları; Bahreyn (geçici); Bender Abbas (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Al Bu Said have governed Oman from the 18th century to the present, establishing a powerful maritime trade empire in Zanzibar. Under Sultan Qaboos (1970-2020), a modern state was built with oil revenues.

raw.region_primary: Anadolu → Güneydoğu Avrupa → Ortadoğu → Kuzey Afrika
raw.regions_all: Söğüt/Bursa (erken); İstanbul; Anadolu; Balkanlar; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Irak; Kuzey Afrika; Kırım
raw.narrative_en: The Ottomans established the longest-lasting and largest empire in Islamic history, spanning three continents over six centuries. Rising from a small Anatolian frontier principality to world power, the Ottomans became heirs to the Roman Empire through the conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the political and religious leader of the Islamic world by assuming the caliphate. Reaching the zenith of law, architecture, and military power under Süleyman the Magnificent, the empire created a distinctive civilizational model through Sinan's masterpieces, the kapıkulu system, and the multi-religious millet structure. This dynasty of 36 sultans exited the historical stage after World War I defeat through the National Struggle and the proclamation of the Republic.

raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Hicaz; Kuzey Irak kısmen; Kilikya; Nübya; Kıbrıs (haraçgüzar)
raw.narrative_en: The Mamluks were a dynasty founded by slave-origin military elites that halted the Mongol invasion at Ayn Jalut and completely expelled the Crusaders from Syria. Throughout the Bahri and Burji periods, they positioned Egypt and Syria as the political and cultural center of the Islamic world, developing a unique system where succession passed not from father to son but to the strongest commander.

raw.regions_all: Mısır; Sudan; Hicaz (geçici); Suriye (geçici); Mora (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Muhammad Ali Dynasty was founded by the Albanian-origin military commander who launched Westernization and modernization in Ottoman Egypt. While radically reforming the army, education, and agriculture, the Suez Canal project elevated Egypt to the center of global trade. The dynasty maintained nominal sovereignty even under British occupation until the 1952 Free Officers coup.

raw.notes: First Islamic dynasty caliphate; Andalusian branch lasted to 1031

names.en: Hāshimite Sharīfs of Mecca from the 'Awn Family

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Hāshimite Sharīfs of Mecca from the 'Awn Family
raw.region_primary: Mekke ve Hicaz; ardından Irak ve Ürdün
raw.regions_all: Mekke; Hicaz; Irak (Faysal kolu); Suriye (geçici); Ürdün (Abdullah kolu)
raw.successor: Hicaz'da Suudi fethi (1344/1925); Irak'ta cumhuriyet (1958); Ürdün'de devam
raw.narrative_en: The Hashimite Sharifs of Mecca, as direct descendants of Prophet Muhammad, governed the Hijaz for centuries and shaped the modern Middle East map through the 1916 Arab Revolt. Though they lost the Hijaz to Saudi conquest, they still reign in Jordan.

raw.regions_all: Sirenaika; Fizan; Çad; Hicaz (zâviyeler)
raw.narrative_en: The Sanusi movement represents a rare transformation from a Sufi order founded by an Algerian-born sheikh into a modern state. Their resistance against Italian occupation formed the basis of Libyan national identity; King Idris gained independence but was overthrown in the 1969 coup.

raw.regions_all: Suhâr; kıyı Umân
raw.narrative_en: The Mukramids were a very short-lived vassal dynasty governing coastal Oman under Buyid suzerainty. Their approximately forty-year period demonstrates the reach of Buyid influence into Oman.

raw.regions_all: Umân (iç bölge ve kıyı); Doğu Afrika kıyıları
raw.narrative_en: The Ya'rubids expelled the Portuguese from Oman's coasts, re-establishing the region's naval power and extending dominion to the East African coast. They collapsed due to internal succession disputes and Iranian intervention.

raw.regions_all: Umân (kıyı ve iç bölgeler)
raw.narrative_en: The Al Julanda were an Azdi Arab family that had ruled Oman since pre-Islamic times, pioneering the first political organization of the Ibadi school in the region during the early Islamic period. Their fall from power through tribal revolts led to the beginning of the Ibadi imamate tradition.

raw.notes: Sharifs ruled Hejaz until Saudi conquest; continued via Jordan/Iraq branches
Cadet branch of House of Saud
Cadet branch of House of Saud; governors of Eastern Province
Former ruling dynasty of Emirate of Jabal Shammar; living descendants in Saudi/Jordan
Current ruling house of Saudi Arabia; ~15000 princes
Senior cadet branch of House of Saud descending from Saud bin Faisal bin Turki
Cadet branch of House of Saud

raw.regions_all: Cebelişemmer; Ha'il; Necd (geçici); Vâdissirhân; Palmira'ya kadar (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Al Rashid ruled northern Najd from Ha'il as chiefs of the Shammar tribe, engaging in a prolonged rivalry with the Saudi family. Though they prospered through caravan trade wealth, they withdrew from the historical stage in 1921 before Ibn Saud's expansion.

raw.regions_all: Dir'iyye; Riyad; Necd; al-Ahsā; Hicaz; Asîr
raw.narrative_en: The Al Saud unified Arabia through their alliance with the Wahhabi movement, passing through three distinct state periods. Abdulaziz Ibn Saud's re-establishment of the kingdom in the early 20th century and the discovery of oil elevated the dynasty to the center of global energy politics.

raw.regions_all: San'a ve çevresi
raw.narrative_en: The Hamdanids of Yemen were a short-lived South Arabian tribal dynasty governing Sana'a after the Sulayhid collapse. They served as a political buffer in the Yemeni highlands until the Ayyubid conquest.

raw.regions_all: Zebîd; Tihâme; Ta'iz (geçici); Aden (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Mahdids were a militant Yemeni dynasty that overthrew the Najahids through a radical preaching movement and established short-lived rule in Zabid. The Ayyubid conquest ended their rapid rise and fall with equal speed.

raw.regions_all: Zebîd; Tihâme
raw.narrative_en: The Najahids were a rare Islamic dynasty of Ethiopian origin, emerging from Ziyadid slave viziers to establish an independent dynasty centered in Zabid in the Tihama. The rise of a Black African slave family to the ruling class in the Islamic world is a remarkable example of social mobility.

raw.parent_tribe: Oğuz Türk (Mencik boyu, Gassânî soy iddiası)
raw.regions_all: Ta'iz; Zebîd; San'a (geçici); Hadramevt; Zufâr
raw.narrative_en: The Rasulids were a dynasty of Turkic origin that inherited the Ayyubid Yemeni legacy, ruling southern Yemen and the Tihama for 230 years and building the region's cultural golden age. They accumulated immense wealth by controlling trade with India, China, and East Africa through the port of Aden. Sultans personally authoring scholarly works created a unique intellectual legacy in the Islamic ruler tradition.

raw.regions_all: San'a; Dhū Cibla; Tihâme; Hicaz (geçici); Aden (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Sulayhids rose as the most successful representatives of the Fatimid Ismaili mission in Yemen, presenting one of Islamic history's most remarkable examples of female governance through al-Sayyida Arwa, nicknamed the 'Second Bilqis.' They laid the foundations for the lasting influence of Ismaili Shiism in Yemen.

raw.regions_all: Aden; Zebîd; San'a (geçici); Ta'iz
raw.narrative_en: The Tahirids were southern Yemeni emirs who gained power as the Rasulid state weakened, defending Aden against the Portuguese threat and representing the region's last independent local dynasty.

raw.regions_all: San'a; Cenad (Janad)
raw.narrative_en: The Yu'firids were the first local South Arabian dynasty based in Sana'a, ruling the Yemeni highlands before the Sulayhids. While maintaining Abbasid allegiance, they provided regional stability, representing an early political expression of Yemeni highland culture.

raw.regions_all: Sa'de; San'a; Yemen yayla bölgesi
raw.narrative_en: The Zaidi Imams of Yemen constituted one of the longest-lived political structures in the Islamic world with an imamate tradition spanning over a millennium. Resisting Sunni and Ismaili rivals in the mountainous geography of Highland Yemen, the Zaidi imams shaped the region's sectarian identity by preserving a distinctive model of moderate Shiism.

raw.regions_all: Zebîd; Tihâme; Hadramevt (kısmen)
raw.narrative_en: The Ziyadids were Yemen's first major local dynasty, founded by a governor sent by the Abbasid caliph, ruling from the Tihama center of Zabid for two centuries. In their later period, power passed to Ethiopian slave viziers, creating an early example of social mobility in Yemeni history.

raw.regions_all: Aden ve çevresi
raw.narrative_en: The Zuray'ids governed the Aden branch of the Sulayhid state, controlling the strategic transit point of international maritime trade as a small but wealthy dynasty. Internal warfare and the growing influence of Ethiopian slave viziers weakened the dynasty, paving the way for the Ayyubid conquest.
